methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
SpaceForest, founded by Robert Magiera, Andrzej Magiera, and Jerzy Michalski, is a Space & Aerospace company that recently completed a public offering (GPW) backed by ESA. The valuation universe comprises 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including SpaceX, Sierra Space, and Planet Labs.

Standard triangulation methods face material constraints here. Peer-median analysis is inapplicable: while 11 same-sector peers exist, filtering for identical stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier yields fewer than the three datapoints required for statistical confidence. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples is similarly blocked—the Space & Aerospace sector median P/S stands at 25.8x across the six public tickers, but SpaceForest's revenue is not on file, precluding multiplication. Private company revenue disclosure remains the limiting factor.

This analysis cannot support a discrete valuation range without either disclosed revenue or a sufficiently dense cohort of same-stage, same-tier private comparables. The key uncertainty is whether SpaceForest's operational and financial profile aligns with SpaceX or the lower-tier private set, a gap that public P/S multiples alone cannot bridge. Practitioners should flag revenue and capital structure as essential inputs before proceeding to multiple-based or DCF methods. The IPO/ESA backing provides credibility but does not substitute for missing operational metrics.
